text: The large emphysematous bulla can be seen incorporating the left upper lobe proper.
The patient is placed under general anesthesia in the right lateral position and three ports are inserted.
They're placed at the 7th intercostal space in the mid-axillary line and two are placed in the 5th intercostal space, one anteriorly and one posteriorly.
Once the left upper lobe is free, you can pass a suction catheter in between the emphysematous bulla, which you can see here on the left side, and to the right of it, this is the spared lingula.
It's clear that you can see the difference in the texture of the tissue.
This clear demarcation line is the line that we would apply the stapler to.
A parietal pleurectomy is performed and this extends anteriorly from the fifth intercostal space port side to posteriorly to the other alternating fifth intercostal space port side.
We perform the pleurectomy using a peanut and some forceps to retract.

You can see here, because the chest tube was placed for a long time, there is some pleural reaction, and this has caused some pleural thickening, and it makes the pleurectomy actually easier to perform.
